explanation:
C++ gives you direct control over memory and hardware — it's the language behind operating systems, game engines, and high-frequency trading. Unlike Python or Java, C++ compiles directly to machine code with no runtime interpreter or garbage collector. You manage memory manually, which means more responsibility but also more performance.

The memory model divides into stack (fast, automatic, local variables) and heap (slow, manual, dynamic allocation). Stack variables are destroyed when they go out of scope. Heap memory persists until you explicitly free it. Understanding this distinction is fundamental to writing correct and efficient C++ code.
key_takeaway: C++ compiles to native code with manual memory management. Stack for automatic variables, heap for dynamic allocation. Always `delete` what you `new`.
try_it: Create a function that takes a `std::vector<int>&` reference and doubles every element in-place.

explanation:
A pointer stores the memory address of another variable — like writing down someone's house address instead of the person themselves. You can follow the address to access or modify the value (dereferencing). References are safer aliases — once bound to a variable, they can't be reseated and can't be null.

Pointers are powerful but dangerous. A dangling pointer points to freed memory. A null pointer points to address 0 (dereferencing crashes). A wild pointer was never initialized. Smart pointers (covered later) eliminate most pointer pitfalls, but understanding raw pointers is essential for reading existing C++ code and understanding what smart pointers do under the hood.
key_takeaway: Pointers store addresses and can be null, dangling, or wild. References are safe aliases that must be initialized and can't be null. Prefer references; use pointers when you need reassignment or optional values.
try_it: Write a function that takes a pointer to an array and its size, and returns the maximum value. Handle the null pointer case.

explanation:
C++ supports classes with constructors, destructors, inheritance, and polymorphism. Unlike Java, C++ supports multiple inheritance (a class can inherit from multiple base classes) and gives you fine-grained control over memory layout. The `virtual` keyword enables runtime polymorphism — calling the correct derived class method through a base class pointer.

The Rule of Three/Five states: if your class manages a resource, you must explicitly define the copy constructor, copy assignment operator, and destructor (Rule of Three), plus move constructor and move assignment operator (Rule of Five). Violating this leads to resource leaks or double-free bugs.
key_takeaway: Use `virtual` for runtime polymorphism, `override` to verify you're overriding, and `= 0` for pure virtual (abstract) methods. Always use virtual destructors in base classes.
try_it: Add a `Triangle` class and implement a `totalPerimeter()` function that sums perimeters of all shapes in the vector.

explanation:
The Standard Template Library (STL) provides generic, tested, high-performance data structures and algorithms. Containers are type-safe templates — `std::vector<int>` and `std::vector<std::string>` are different types generated from the same template. The three main sequence containers are `vector` (dynamic array), `list` (doubly-linked list), and `deque` (double-ended queue).

Associative containers (`set`, `map`) use red-black trees for O(log n) lookups with sorted order. Unordered containers (`unordered_set`, `unordered_map`) use hash tables for O(1) average lookups without ordering. Choosing the right container depends on your access patterns: random access → vector, frequent insertions in the middle → list, fast key lookups → unordered_map.
key_takeaway: Use `vector` as the default container. `map`/`set` for sorted data, `unordered_map`/`unordered_set` for O(1) lookups. STL algorithms (`sort`, `find`, `count_if`) work with all containers.
try_it: Create a word frequency counter using `unordered_map<std::string, int>` that counts occurrences of each word in a sentence.

explanation:
Templates are C++'s mechanism for generic programming — writing code that works with any type. The compiler generates a specialized version for each type you use, so there's zero runtime cost. Think of templates as a recipe that works with any ingredient — the same `sort` function sorts integers, strings, or custom objects.

Function templates deduce types automatically. Class templates create generic data structures (like `std::vector<T>`). Template specialization lets you provide custom implementations for specific types. C++20 concepts add constraints to templates, giving clear error messages when types don't meet requirements.
key_takeaway: Templates enable zero-cost generic programming. The compiler generates specialized code for each type. C++20 concepts add type constraints for clearer error messages.
try_it: Create a generic `Pair<T1, T2>` template class with `first` and `second` members, and a `swap()` method that swaps the two values.

explanation:
RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) is C++'s most important idiom: resources (memory, file handles, locks) are tied to object lifetimes. When an object is created, it acquires the resource. When it's destroyed (goes out of scope), the destructor releases it. This eliminates resource leaks without garbage collection.

The problem with raw `new`/`delete` is that exceptions, early returns, or forgotten `delete` calls leak memory. RAII solves this: use `std::vector` instead of `new int[]`, `std::fstream` instead of `fopen`/`fclose`, and `std::lock_guard` instead of manual `mutex.lock()`/`mutex.unlock()`.
key_takeaway: RAII ties resource lifetime to object lifetime. When the object is destroyed, the resource is released. Use smart pointers, vectors, lock_guards, and fstreams instead of raw resource management.
try_it: Create a `ScopedTimer` RAII class that prints elapsed time in its destructor when it goes out of scope.

explanation:
Move semantics (C++11) eliminate unnecessary copies. Instead of duplicating a large vector, you "move" it — transferring ownership of the underlying memory to the new owner, leaving the original in a valid but empty state. This is like handing someone a box instead of photocopying everything inside it.

Smart pointers automate memory management through RAII. `unique_ptr` owns memory exclusively — only one pointer can own the resource. `shared_ptr` uses reference counting — multiple pointers share ownership, and the last one to die frees the memory. `weak_ptr` observes a `shared_ptr` without affecting the reference count, preventing circular references.
key_takeaway: Use `unique_ptr` for exclusive ownership, `shared_ptr` for shared ownership with reference counting, and `weak_ptr` to break circular references. Move semantics eliminate expensive copies.
try_it: Create a linked list using `unique_ptr<Node>` for `next` pointers. Implement `push_front`, `pop_front`, and `print` methods.

explanation:
C++ interview problems test your mastery of memory management, pointers, and the STL. The most critical patterns involve raw pointer manipulation for linked lists and trees, using iterators with STL containers, and understanding copy/move semantics. C++ interviews uniquely test your knowledge of undefined behavior, const correctness, and RAII.

The STL is your best friend: `std::vector` for dynamic arrays, `std::unordered_map` for hash maps, `std::set`/`std::map` for ordered containers, and `std::priority_queue` for heaps. Know their iterator invalidation rules and time complexities. For string problems, prefer `std::string` over C-style char arrays. Smart pointers (`unique_ptr`, `shared_ptr`) demonstrate modern C++ knowledge.
key_takeaway: Master `unordered_map` for O(1) lookups and `vector` for dynamic arrays — they form the foundation of most C++ interview solutions.
try_it: Solve Two Sum using unordered_map, then try implementing it with a sorted vector and two pointers.

practice_challenges.title: Two Sum
practice_challenges.hint: Use `unordered_map<int,int>` to store value-to-index. Check `find()` for the complement at each step.
practice_challenges.description: Given an array of integers and a target, return indices of two numbers that add up to the target.

practice_challenges.title: Valid Parentheses
practice_challenges.hint: Use `std::stack<char>`. Push opening brackets, pop and compare on closing brackets.
practice_challenges.description: Given a string of brackets, determine if the input is valid (properly opened and closed).

practice_challenges.title: Container With Most Water
practice_challenges.hint: Start with pointers at both ends. Move the shorter pointer inward — area = min(height[left], height[right]) * (right - left).
practice_challenges.description: Given n vertical lines, find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container holding the most water.

practice_challenges.title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
practice_challenges.hint: Maintain a `vector<int> tails` where tails[i] is the smallest tail of all increasing subsequences of length i+1. Use `lower_bound` for O(n log n).
practice_challenges.description: Given an integer array, find the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.

practice_challenges.title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
practice_challenges.hint: Binary search on the partition of the shorter array. Find a split where maxLeft <= minRight on both sides.
practice_challenges.description: Given two sorted arrays, find the median of the combined sorted array in O(log(min(m,n))) time.
